title: Step 01 – Getting Started with Vite + React + TypeScript
summary: Hey interns! Welcome to Step 1 — we just scaffolded our very first React + TypeScript app using Vite. Ran the dev server, saw the spinning logo, and confirmed Hot Module Replacement works like a charm. It's not much yet, but every skyscraper starts with a foundation (or at least a `package.json`). Why do programmers prefer dark mode? Because light attracts bugs! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 02 – Clean Home & About Page
summary: What's up interns! In Step 2 we gutted the Vite boilerplate and replaced it with a clean Home page and an About Me section using nothing but semantic HTML — no className in sight. It feels good to start fresh. You know, a clean codebase is like a clean desk… it lasts about five minutes. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 03 – Header with Profile Photo
summary: Yo interns! Step 3 is all about adding a profile image to the header from the public folder. We learned about root-relative paths and proper alt text for accessibility. Fun fact: I spent 10 minutes picking the right photo before realizing nobody looks good in 100×100 pixels. Why did the developer go broke? Because he used up all his cache! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 04 – Basic Routing with One Link
summary: Hey team! In Step 4 we installed react-router-dom and set up HashRouter so we can navigate between Home and About without a full-page reload. One link, one route, one giant leap for our little app. Why do Java developers wear glasses? Because they can't C#. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 05 – Extract Simple Components
summary: What's good interns! Step 5 was all about refactoring — we broke that monolithic App.tsx into Header, AboutSection, and Footer components in their own files. Same UI, way better code organization. It's like cleaning your room: nobody notices but you feel amazing. What's a programmer's favorite hangout place? Foo Bar! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 06 – Static Entries List
summary: Hey there interns! In Step 6 we added an All Entries page that renders hardcoded dev-log entries through EntryCard components. We practiced typed data modules and Array.map() — the bread and butter of React lists. Why do programmers hate nature? It has too many bugs and no documentation. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 07 – Controlled Form Basics
summary: Sup interns! Step 7 — we built a New Entry form using React controlled inputs with useState, value, and onChange. Submission logs to console and resets the fields. It's alive! Well, sort of. It console.logs. Baby steps. What's the object-oriented way to become wealthy? Inheritance! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 08 – Form Validation (Minimal)
summary: Hey interns! In Step 8 we added client-side validation to our form — both fields required, inline errors after first submit, disabled submit button while invalid, plus aria-invalid and aria-describedby for a11y. Because friends don't let friends ship forms without validation. Why did the developer quit his job? Because he didn't get arrays! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 09 – State Lifting & Props
summary: What's up interns! Step 9 is the big one — we lifted the entries array into App state, passed it down via props, and wired an onAddEntry callback to the form. New entries actually show up on the list now. The app is interactive for the first time! You know what they say: props go down, callbacks go up, and bugs go everywhere. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 10 – Tags & Mood Fields
summary: Yo interns! In Step 10 we extended the Entry type with a mood (union type via select), tags (comma-separated string turned into an array), and a createdAt timestamp. The form and card both got upgrades. Our entries have feelings now — literally. How many programmers does it take to change a light bulb? None, that's a hardware problem! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 11 – Express API Scaffold
summary: Hey interns! Welcome to the backend! In Step 11 we created a standalone Express 5 + TypeScript server with a /api/health endpoint, .env configuration, startup guards, and CORS. No database yet — just a heartbeat. It's like building a car engine before you have wheels. What's a backend developer's favorite type of music? Heavy server metal! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 12 – Prisma + SQL Server Setup
summary: What's good interns! Step 12 — we plugged in Prisma ORM connected to SQL Server, defined the Entry model, pushed the schema to create the table, and added a /api/health/ready endpoint that pings the database. We have a real database now! I asked my database out to dinner. It said it already had too many connections. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 13 – CRUD Entries
summary: Hey team! In Step 13 we implemented all five REST endpoints — GET list, GET by id, POST, PUT, and DELETE — using an Express Router and Prisma queries, with request validation and proper HTTP status codes. Full CRUD, baby! A SQL query walks into a bar, sees two tables and asks: 'Can I JOIN you?' Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 14 – Frontend Fetch: List & Create
summary: Sup interns! Step 14 — we connected the React frontend to the Express API! Replaced seed data with fetch + useEffect calls, added a Vite proxy for /api, and wired the New Entry form to POST to the server. Frontend and backend are talking now. Why do frontend devs eat lunch alone? Because they don't know how to make server requests… until now! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 15 – Edit & Delete Flows
summary: Hey interns! In Step 15 we added edit and delete functionality with a reusable form component, PUT/DELETE fetch calls, optimistic UI updates, rollback on error, and toast notifications. Users can now modify and remove entries. With great CRUD power comes great CRUD responsibility. What's a ninja programmer's weapon of choice? Throwing stars on GitHub! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 16 – Filtering by Tags
summary: What's up interns! Step 16 — we added tag-based filtering with a GET /api/entries/tags endpoint, a Prisma contains query with a database index, and a TagFilter dropdown that syncs with useSearchParams. Filter your chaos! Why couldn't the developer find his favorite tag? Because it wasn't indexed! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 17 – Pagination & Sorting
summary: Yo interns! In Step 17 we implemented server-side pagination with skip/take, count queries, and sortable columns with a whitelist. The API returns a nice { data, page, limit, total, totalPages } envelope, and the frontend got Pagination and SortControls components synced to URL params. Why did the pagination go to therapy? It had too many issues to skip. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 18 – Auth Basics
summary: Hey interns! Step 18 is a big milestone — we added user authentication with bcrypt password hashing, JWT tokens, Express auth middleware, a React AuthContext with localStorage persistence, protected routes via Navigate, and a Login/Register page. Security matters! I told my password it was too weak. It didn't take it personally, it didn't have enough characters. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 19 – Deploy Backend
summary: What's good interns! In Step 19 we made the server production-ready by adding morgan request logging, serving the Vite build as static files, graceful SIGTERM/SIGINT shutdown, and a start script for hosting platforms. Our baby is almost ready for the real world. Why do servers never get lonely? Because they're always listening! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 20 – Deploy Frontend to GitHub Pages
summary: Hey team! Step 20 — we deployed the React frontend to GitHub Pages using a configurable Vite base path, VITE_* env vars for the API URL, and the gh-pages package for one-command deploys. The world can see our app now! My code doesn't have bugs, it just develops random features on GitHub Pages. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 21 – Testing & CI
summary: Sup interns! In Step 21 we added automated tests with Vitest and React Testing Library for components, supertest for API endpoints, and a GitHub Actions CI workflow that runs all tests on every push. Tests are the seatbelt of coding — annoying until they save your life. Why do testers love the fall? Because of all the edge cases! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 22 – Accessibility & Polish
summary: Hey interns! Step 22 was all about a11y love — skip-to-content link, focus management on route changes, NavLink with aria-current, focus-visible outlines, toast live regions, focus-first-invalid on form errors, color-contrast fixes, and a 404 page. Accessibility isn't optional, it's essential. What did the ARIA label say to the button? 'Don't worry, I've got your role covered.' Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 23 – Tailwind + daisyUI Setup
summary: What's up interns! In Step 23 we installed Tailwind CSS v4 and daisyUI v5, configured the Vite plugin, and restyled the layout shell — navbar, footer, and containers — with daisyUI classes while keeping all our a11y features intact. Our app finally looks like it was made this decade. Why did CSS break up with HTML? Because it found a better class with Tailwind. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 24 – daisyUI Components
summary: Yo interns! Step 24 — we restyled every remaining component with daisyUI classes: entry cards, forms, pagination, filters, sort controls, toasts, and the login page. No bare inline styles or unstyled HTML left standing. The glow-up is real. Why do CSS developers never feel lonely? Because they always have great class. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 25 – Prisma MySQL Migration
summary: Hey interns! In Step 25 we switched the database from SQL Server to MySQL/MariaDB by updating the Prisma provider, translating native column types, and generating/applying migration SQL. Zero changes to routes or frontend — that's the beauty of an ORM layer. I wanted to switch databases, but my SQL Server had attachment issues. DROP TABLE fears! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 26 – Deploy Backend to NFSN
summary: What's good interns! Final step! In Step 26 we deployed the Express backend to NearlyFreeSpeech.NET on FreeBSD, replaced Prisma with raw mysql2 queries (no FreeBSD Prisma binary), configured a daemon and Apache proxy, and went fully live. We did it! The whole thing, start to finish, front to back. Why did the server move to FreeBSD? It wanted to be free… as in nearly free speech. Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Bonus – Dummy Data & Demo Mode for GitHub Pages
summary: Hey interns! We just added a full demo mode so the GitHub Pages version works without a backend. Created dummy-logs.json with a log entry for every step, dummy-users.json with sample credentials, and a DemoData layer that uses localStorage to cache new entries. The api/entries.ts and api/auth.ts files now auto-detect when VITE_API_URL is missing and route everything through the demo layer. Why did the JSON file break up with the database? It needed more space! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Bonus – Demo Credentials on Login Page
summary: Sup interns! Quick UX win — we added a credentials table to the login page that only shows up in demo mode. Now anyone visiting the GitHub Pages version can see the sample usernames and passwords right there, no guessing required. It's conditionally rendered with a simple DEMO flag check. What did the login form say to the user? 'I promise I won't judge your password.' Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Bonus – Accordion Entry Cards & Oldest-First Sort
summary: Yo interns! We swapped out the full card layout for daisyUI accordion/collapse components — now you can see way more entries on screen and expand whichever one you want to read. Also flipped the default sort from newest-first to oldest-first so the entries flow like a story from Step 1 to Step 26. Why did the accordion go to therapy? It had too many things bottled up inside! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Bonus – Responsive Navbar with Hamburger Menu
summary: What's up interns! We made the navbar responsive — on desktop you get the classic horizontal text links, and on mobile it collapses into a hamburger menu dropdown with SVG icons next to each link. Home gets a house, Entries gets a document, New Entry gets a plus, About gets an info circle, and Log In/Out get door arrows. Clean and compact. Why did the hamburger menu feel so important? Because it held everything together! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 31 – Show Entry Author
summary: Hey interns! In Step 31 we added a LEFT JOIN between Entry and User so every API response now includes an author field with the username of whoever created it. On the frontend, EntryCard conditionally renders 'by username' right next to the date. Entries without a userId gracefully show nothing — that's the beauty of LEFT JOIN. Why did the SQL query break up with the subquery? It found a better JOIN! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 32 – Add Email to User
summary: Sup interns! Step 32 — we added an email column to the User table with a unique constraint, updated the registration endpoint to require and validate email, and added an email field to the frontend registration form. The demo layer and dummy data got updated too. Now every user has an email on file — one step closer to verification. Why did the email go to therapy? It had too many attachment issues! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv

title: Step 33 – Social Schema Migrations
summary: Hey interns! Step 33 is our prep step — we added handle, displayName, bio, and avatarUrl to the User table, a visibility column to Entry, and created the Friendship table. No new UI yet — just laying the foundation for profiles, friend requests, and private entries. Think of it like pouring concrete before building the house. Why did the database administrator leave his wife? She had too many one-to-many relationships! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv
author: jv

title: Step 34 – User Profiles Backend
summary: Hey interns! Step 34 — we built the profile API! GET /api/users/:handle looks up any user by their handle slug, and PUT /api/users/me/profile lets you update your own displayName, bio, and avatarUrl. The demo layer got matching functions so GitHub Pages still works offline. Why do back-end developers make great DJs? Because they know how to handle requests! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv
author: jv

title: Step 35 – User Profiles Frontend
summary: Hey interns! Step 35 — we built the profile UI! There's now a /u/:handle route that shows a profile card with the user's display name, bio, avatar, and join date. If you're looking at your own profile you get an Edit button that opens an inline form. The Header links your name to your profile page too. Why did the React component break up with the profile page? It said 'You're not my type!' Keep coding, keep reading ~jv
author: jv

title: Step 36 -- Entry Visibility
summary: Hey interns! Step 36 -- we added a visibility selector to the entry form so you can mark entries as Public, Friends only, or Private. The card now shows a little lock or people icon when it's not public. The backend accepts and stores the visibility value on create and update too. Why did the private entry refuse to go to the party? Because it didn't want to be seen in public! Keep coding, keep reading ~jv
author: jv
